title: Should you turn radiators off in unused rooms?
description: Turning radiators down in unused rooms saves roughly £26 to £95 a year at the 2026 cap, but done badly it invites mould. Here is the right way to zone.
blurb: What turning off spare-room radiators really saves, when it backfires with mould, and the valve setting to use instead.
lede: Every gas-heated home in Britain has at least one room that gets heated for no good reason. The spare bedroom that hosts a guest twice a year, the dining room that became a dumping ground, the box room full of Christmas decorations. Heating rooms nobody sits in feels wasteful, and broadly it is, so turning those radiators off looks like free money. Mostly it is. But the full answer has more moving parts than the valve on the radiator: heat leaks between rooms, cold rooms grow mould, and the right approach for a gas boiler is close to the opposite of the right approach for a heat pump. This guide puts real numbers on the saving, explains when switching radiators off backfires, and shows you how to do it without damp patches appearing on the walls by February.
short_answer: Yes, in most gas-heated homes turning radiators down or off in rooms you rarely use saves money, typically £26 to £95 a year depending on how many rooms and how big they are, at the July to September 2026 gas cap of 7.33p per kWh. Turn the thermostatic valve down to a low or frost setting rather than fully off, keep the door closed, and keep an eye out for condensation. If you have a heat pump, the advice flips: most run more efficiently heating the whole house gently.

sections.h2: How much does turning off a radiator actually save?
sections.html:
Start with how the heating bill splits up. A typical gas-heated three-bed semi uses around 11,500 kWh of gas a year (Ofgem's typical consumption figure), and roughly 80 to 85 per cent of that goes on space heating, with the rest on hot water. That puts space heating at roughly 9,200 to 9,800 kWh a year, or about £675 to £720 at the current gas unit rate.

Each room is responsible for a slice of that, roughly in proportion to its share of the heat loss: its floor area, how many external walls it has, and how well it is insulated and glazed. A small box room on an internal corner might account for 5 per cent of the house's heat demand. A large bedroom with two external walls might be 10 to 12 per cent.

You do not save the whole slice by turning the radiator off, though, and this is the bit most articles skip. An unheated room inside a heated house is not sealed off. Heat flows into it through internal walls, the floor, the ceiling and around the door, because internal walls have little or no insulation. The unheated room settles a few degrees below the rest of the house, and your boiler quietly pays to keep it there. In practice you recover perhaps half to two-thirds of the room's nominal share, more if it is a well-separated room with the door kept shut, less if it is open to a landing.

Putting that together for a typical gas-heated semi over a full heating season:

Room turned down for the heating seasonEstimated gas saved per yearEstimated saving per year
Small box room or single bedroom (about 8 m²)350 to 500 kWh£26 to £37
Standard double bedroom (about 12 m²)550 to 800 kWh£40 to £59
Large bedroom or little-used dining room (15 m² plus)800 to 1,100 kWh£59 to £81
Two spare rooms together900 to 1,300 kWh£66 to £95

At 7.33p per kWh for gas, Ofgem cap July to September 2026. Estimates assume a gas-heated three-bed semi, radiators set to low or frost protection for the full heating season, and doors kept closed. Detached, draughty or poorly insulated homes will save more; flats with heated neighbours either side will save less.

So the honest headline is tens of pounds per room per year, not hundreds. That is still a genuinely free saving for rooms you truly do not use, and it stacks with the bigger levers like your thermostat settings and turning your boiler flow temperature down, which can each save considerably more.

sections.h2: Why turning the valve to low beats turning it off
sections.html:
If your radiators have thermostatic radiator valves (TRVs), the numbered dials, the best move is almost never the fully off position. Turn the valve down to its lowest numbered setting, or to the frost symbol if it has one, rather than to zero. Three reasons:

  • Frost protection. The frost setting (usually a snowflake symbol) opens the valve if the room approaches around 5°C. That protects the room's pipework and plumbing in a cold snap. A fully closed valve protects nothing, and a burst pipe in an unused room can leak for days before anyone notices.
  • Protecting the building fabric. A room held at 10 to 14°C stays reasonably dry and stable. A room allowed to drop to outdoor-adjacent temperatures cycles through cold and damp, which is hard on plaster, timber windows and anything stored in there.
  • Valve health. TRV pins can stick if left hard closed for months. A valve sitting at a low setting still moves occasionally and is far less likely to seize. If you do shut valves fully for the summer, it is good practice to open and close them a few times when the heating season starts.

On setting numbers: most TRV scales run from * (frost) through 1 to 5. Setting 1 typically holds a room somewhere around 12 to 15°C and is the sensible choice for an unused room. Setting 2 or 3 suits rooms you use occasionally. There is a fuller explanation of how the valves work, and how to zone a house room by room, in our guide to radiator valves and zoning.

One more practical point: keep the door of the unheated room closed. An open door turns the room back into part of the heated volume and erases most of the saving. A cheap draught excluder along the bottom of the door helps too, both for the saving and for keeping warm moist air out of the cold room, which matters for the next section.

This is the genuine downside, and the reason "just turn it all off" is bad advice. Warm air holds more moisture than cold air. The air in your home carries moisture from cooking, showers, drying laundry and simply breathing. When that air drifts into a cold room and touches a cold surface, an external wall, a window reveal, the back of a wardrobe, it cools below its dew point and the moisture condenses out onto the surface. Do that daily for a few months and you get black mould, usually first in corners and behind furniture where air barely moves.

An unheated spare room is the classic place this happens, because it combines cold surfaces with still air and a closed door. The fix is not to abandon the idea but to manage it:

  • Keep the room at a low background temperature rather than stone cold. TRV setting 1, or roughly 12 to 14°C, keeps surfaces above the dew point in most homes.
  • Keep the door closed so moist air from the rest of the house does not migrate in, and never dry laundry in an unheated room.
  • Ventilate briefly but regularly. Opening the window for five to ten minutes a couple of times a week swaps the moist air for drier outside air without meaningfully cooling the fabric of the room.
  • Pull furniture a few centimetres off external walls so air can circulate behind it.
  • Check the room monthly through winter. Misted windows in the morning are your early warning sign.

If you spot trouble starting, deal with it early. Our guide to cheap fixes for condensation and mould covers the low-cost options, from moisture traps to where a small dehumidifier earns its keep. A patch of mould remediation can cost more than the radiator saving for several winters, so this caveat deserves respect rather than panic.

sections.h2: Gas boiler or heat pump? The advice is different
sections.html:
Everything above assumes a gas boiler, where the logic is simple: less heat emitted means less gas burned. Two system-level details are worth knowing, and one big exception.

With a gas boiler

Closing radiators slightly reduces the volume of the heating circuit. Modern boilers and systems handle this fine, but if you shut off several radiators in a small system you may notice the boiler short-cycling, switching on and off rapidly, because the remaining radiators cannot shed heat fast enough. If that happens, reopen one radiator or ask a heating engineer about the bypass and pump settings. It is also worth rebalancing afterwards if some rooms now heat noticeably faster than others.

There is also a small efficiency bonus available: with fewer radiators calling for heat, the remaining ones often have spare capacity, which means you can run a lower flow temperature and keep a condensing boiler in its most efficient range. That pairs naturally with turning rooms down, and the flow temperature change alone is often worth more than the closed radiators.

With a heat pump

Here the standard advice flips. Heat pumps deliver their best efficiency running long, steady and low, with water temperatures far below what a boiler uses, and most are designed around the whole house as one open zone. Closing radiators raises the temperature the unit has to produce for the remaining rooms and reduces the system volume it needs to run smoothly, both of which hurt efficiency. Many installers explicitly advise leaving all radiators open and letting weather compensation do the work. If you have one, or are considering one, see our guide to how heat pumps work before zoning anything off, and ask your installer what the system was designed around.

The same one-room logic also has a bigger cousin: if you spend your evenings in a single room, it can be cheaper to heat that room directly and let the rest of the house idle. We run those numbers in whether it is cheaper to heat one room or the whole house.

sections.h2: How to do it properly in five steps
sections.html:
The whole job takes about fifteen minutes and costs nothing if your radiators already have TRVs.

  • 1. Decide which rooms genuinely qualify. Unused means unused: a guest room, a storage room, a study you abandoned. A bathroom does not qualify (you want it warm and dry), and neither does a room someone sleeps in, even briefly each day.
  • 2. Set the valves low, not off. Frost setting or 1 on the TRV dial. If a radiator has only a plain manual valve, close it most of the way rather than fully, or consider fitting a TRV, which costs around £10 to £25 per valve plus fitting.
  • 3. Close the doors and draught-proof them. The saving lives or dies on this. A brush strip or fabric excluder on the door bottom costs a few pounds.
  • 4. Set a ventilation habit. A few minutes of open window twice a week, and a monthly glance at corners, window reveals and behind furniture.
  • 5. Reverse it when life changes. Guests coming for the weekend? Turn the valve to 3 the day before they arrive. A cold-soaked room takes several hours to feel properly warm, because the furniture and walls need to warm up, not just the air.

While you are kneeling at the valves, check whether any radiators need bleeding, since a radiator that is cold at the top wastes heat in the rooms you do use. Our guide to bleeding radiators covers it in two minutes flat.

Turn them down, not off, and yes, do it. For a typical gas-heated home, each genuinely unused room set to a frost or low setting saves roughly £26 to £81 a year at current prices, with two spare rooms together approaching £95. The savings are real but modest, so treat this as one item on the checklist rather than the whole plan: lowering the boiler flow temperature, sensible thermostat scheduling and basic draught-proofing each save as much or more, and they all stack.

The two rules that keep it from backfiring: hold unused rooms at a low background temperature rather than letting them go stone cold, and watch for condensation through the first winter. And if your home runs on a heat pump, leave the radiators open unless your installer tells you otherwise, because zoning a heat pump usually costs more in lost efficiency than it saves in unheated rooms.

For the rest of the picture, from radiator reflectors to what each degree on the dial costs, our heating guides cover every option with the same real-number approach.

faq.q: How much money does turning off one radiator save?
faq.a: For a typical gas-heated home, roughly £26 to £81 a year per room at the July to September 2026 gas cap of 7.33p per kWh, depending on the room's size and how exposed it is. A small internal box room sits at the bottom of that range, a large room with two external walls at the top. You save less than the room's full share of the heating bill because heat leaks into unheated rooms through internal walls and floors.

faq.q: Is it bad to turn radiators completely off?
faq.a: Fully off carries three risks: no frost protection for pipework in a cold snap, condensation and mould as the room goes cold and damp, and TRV pins that can stick after months hard closed. Setting the valve to its frost symbol or lowest number avoids all three while keeping nearly all of the saving.

faq.q: Does turning off radiators in unused rooms cause damp?
faq.a: It can if the room goes properly cold. Moist air from the rest of the house condenses on cold surfaces, and mould follows within weeks. Prevent it by holding the room at roughly 12 to 14 degrees, keeping the door closed, never drying laundry in there, and airing the room briefly a couple of times a week.

faq.q: Should I turn radiators off in unused rooms if I have a heat pump?
faq.a: Usually not. Heat pumps run most efficiently heating the whole house steadily at a low water temperature, and closing radiators forces the unit to run hotter and less efficiently for the remaining rooms. Most installers recommend leaving all radiators open. Ask whoever designed your system before zoning rooms off.

faq.q: Does closing radiator valves damage the boiler?
faq.a: Closing one or two radiators is harmless on a modern system. Shutting off many radiators in a small system can make the boiler short-cycle because the water volume drops and the remaining radiators cannot shed heat fast enough. If you hear rapid on-off firing after closing several valves, reopen one or ask a heating engineer to check the bypass arrangement.
